(born 1967 in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), [England](/wiki/England "England")) is a [Canadian](/wiki/Canadians "Canadians") creator and producer for film and television, a published author, public speaker, journalist, and former broadcaster.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Canadian film director and writer

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Please help by adding [reliable sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources"). | Zarqa Nawaz | |

| Zarqa Nawaz is inducted into the Brampton Arts Walk of Fame | |
| Born | 1967 (age 58–59)  [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), [England](/wiki/England "England") |
| Occupations | [Journalist](/wiki/Journalist "Journalist"), [broadcaster](/wiki/News_presenter "News presenter"), [filmmaker](/wiki/Filmmaker "Filmmaker"), [writer](/wiki/Writer "Writer"), [director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), [producer](/wiki/Film_producer "Film producer") |

**Zarqa Nawaz** (born 1967 in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), [England](/wiki/England "England")) is a [Canadian](/wiki/Canadians "Canadians") creator and producer for film and television, a published author, public speaker, journalist, and former broadcaster.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zarqa_Nawaz&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Zarqa Nawaz was raised in the [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") area and attended Chinguacousy Secondary School.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Initially, Nawaz planned to go to medical school and completed a Bachelor of Science degree from the University of Toronto. She completed a second degree in journalism at [Ryerson Polytechnical Institute](/wiki/Ryerson_Polytechnical_Institute "Ryerson Polytechnical Institute") in 1992. She worked with [CBC Radio](/wiki/CBC_Radio "CBC Radio"), [CBC Newsworld](/wiki/CBC_Newsworld "CBC Newsworld"), [CBC Television](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television")'s *[The National](/wiki/CBC_News:_The_National "CBC News: The National")*, and [CTV](/wiki/CTV_television_network "CTV television network")'s *[Canada AM](/wiki/Canada_AM "Canada AM")*, and was an associate producer of several CBC Radio programs including *[Morningside](/wiki/Morningside_(radio_program) "Morningside (radio program)")*. Her 1992 radio documentary *The Changing Rituals of Death* won multiple awards at the Ontario Telefest Awards. Stating that she became "bored of journalism," she took a summer film workshop at the [Ontario College of Art & Design](/wiki/Ontario_College_of_Art_%26_Design "Ontario College of Art & Design") and began working as a filmmaker, using comedy to explore the relationships between Muslims and their neighbours in contemporary North America. She has described the goal of her production company, FUNdamentalist Films, as "putting the 'fun' back into fundamentalism."

In a 2003 interview with *Prairie Dog Magazine*, Nawaz said her screenplay *Real Terrorists Don't Bellydance* was "inspired by movies like *[True Lies](/wiki/True_Lies "True Lies")* and *[Executive Decision](/wiki/Executive_Decision "Executive Decision").*" She describes it as a "new genre of film," a cross between a terrorist flick and a comedy: "I call it a 'terrordy.'" [[2]](#cite_note-2) Her use of humour in the television series *[Little Mosque on the Prairie](/wiki/Little_Mosque_on_the_Prairie "Little Mosque on the Prairie")* attracted media attention ranging from [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN") and *[The Jerusalem Post](/wiki/The_Jerusalem_Post "The Jerusalem Post")* to *[The Colbert Report](/wiki/The_Colbert_Report "The Colbert Report")* even before it aired, prompting the CBC to broadcast it months ahead of its original schedule.

In 2007, Nawaz created the internationally renowned CBC comedy series, *[Little Mosque on the Prairie](/wiki/Little_Mosque_on_the_Prairie "Little Mosque on the Prairie")*, the world's first sitcom about a Muslim community living in the west. *Little Mosque on the Prairie* premiered to the highest ratings CBC ever had.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The show went on to win a Gemini,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and was nominated for Best Television Series – Comedy at the 2007 [Directors Guild of Canada](/wiki/Directors_Guild_of_Canada "Directors Guild of Canada") Awards. Internationally, *Little Mosque* won awards for Best International Television Series and Best Screenplay at the 2007 Roma Fiction Fest. In 2012, it made its American debut on [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu"), and is currently streaming on Amazon Prime. The *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* said: "the genius of 'Mosque' is that the characters resonate with viewers all over the world."[[5]](#cite_note-5)
Nawaz's CBC show *Little Mosque on the Prairie* was inspired by her documentary *[Me and the Mosque](/wiki/Me_and_the_Mosque "Me and the Mosque")*. She felt that mosques would be run differently if imams were recruited from North America instead of being brought from overseas where cultural differences, especially when it came to women, affected how the imams behaved with their congregation.

After the success of *Little Mosque on the Prairie*, Nawaz successfully sold comedy pilots to ABC, CBS, NBC and Fox Studios.

Nawaz's memoir *Laughing All the Way to the Mosque*,[[6]](#cite_note-6) published in 2014, was a shortlisted nominee for the 2015 [Stephen Leacock Award](/wiki/Stephen_Leacock_Award "Stephen Leacock Award").[[7]](#cite_note-fallis2015-7)
The book was also nominated for the Kobo Emerging Writer's Award,[[8]](#cite_note-8) Saskatchewan Book Award of the Year,[[9]](#cite_note-9) and *[Chatelaine](/wiki/Chatelaine_(magazine) "Chatelaine (magazine)")* magazine's Book Club Pick.[[10]](#cite_note-10) "With a light touch and great humour," wrote reviewer Joanne Latimer in *[Maclean's](/wiki/Maclean%27s "Maclean's")* magazine, "she pokes fun at Muslims and her Punjabi-Canadian clan. The anecdotes create a wry portrait of an immigrant daughter struggling against tradition, sexism and the shalwar kameez."

Nawaz was also as an advice columnist for the *[Globe & Mail](/wiki/Globe_%26_Mail "Globe & Mail")*[[11]](#cite_note-11) from February 2012 to August 2012. In 2018, she was the host of *The Morning Edition*, Saskatchewan's CBC Radio's morning show,[[12]](#cite_note-12) and in 2019 she was the anchor of CBC Saskatchewan's six o'clock news.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Nawaz received a Doctor of Divinity from the University of Saskatchewan for her interfaith work in the community. In recognition for her contribution to the arts, she was inducted into Brampton Arts Walk of Fame in 2019.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Nawaz started doing stand-up in 2020 and co-created the first Women and Non-Binary Open Mic[[15]](#cite_note-15) in Regina where she headlined on opening night at The Cure. Prior to the COVID outbreak in March 2020, Nawaz did weekly open mics at The Cure, and monthly open mics at The Exchange, The Fat Badger, the Creative City Centre and The Social LOL in Regina, Saskatchewan.

In 2021, CBC Television announced the production of *[Zarqa](/wiki/Zarqa_(web_series) "Zarqa (web series)")*, a web series in which Nawaz stars as a divorced Muslim woman reentering the dating scene.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The series premiered on [CBC Gem](/wiki/CBC_Gem "CBC Gem") in May 2022.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Nawaz's novel *Jameela Green Ruins Everything* was also published in 2022,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and was a shortlisted Leacock Award finalist in 2023.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Zarqa Nawaz is the mother of four children and lives in [Regina, Saskatchewan](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan").

## Short films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zarqa_Nawaz&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Short films")]

* *BBQ Muslims* (1995) – Two Muslim brothers are accused of terrorism after their [barbecue](/wiki/Barbecue "Barbecue") explodes in their backyard.
* *Death Threat* (1998) – A young Muslim novelist claims to have received a death threat in order to get her book published.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* *Random Check* (2005) – A young man, late for his wedding, turns to the media after being arrested at the airport as a result of [racial profiling](/wiki/Racial_profiling "Racial profiling").[[21]](#cite_note-auto-21)
* *Fred's Burqa* (2005) – A stolen burqa leads to mistaken identity, a career change and true love.[[21]](#cite_note-auto-21)

## Screenplays

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zarqa_Nawaz&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Screenplays")]

* *Real Terrorists Don't Bellydance* (2003) – a struggling actor inadvertently takes a role as a stereotypical Muslim terrorist, to his fiancée's chagrin.

## Feature-length films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zarqa_Nawaz&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Feature-length films")]

* *[Me and the Mosque](/wiki/Me_and_the_Mosque "Me and the Mosque")* (2005) – documentary about the role of women in Islam, both throughout history and in contemporary Canada, told from a personal perspective.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Television series (creator/writer)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zarqa_Nawaz&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Television series (creator/writer)")]

* *[Little Mosque on the Prairie](/wiki/Little_Mosque_on_the_Prairie "Little Mosque on the Prairie")* (2007-2012) – comedy about relations between Muslims and non-Muslims living in a fictional Saskatchewan town called Mercy.
* *Zarqa* (2022–Present) - comedy about a middle-aged Muslim divorcee who is looking to one-up her ex after finding out he is marrying a white yoga instructor half his age.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zarqa_Nawaz&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: See also")]

* [List of Pakistani journalists](/wiki/List_of_Pakistani_journalists "List of Pakistani journalists")
